In surgery, to perform some manual act in a methodical manner upon a human body, and usually with instruments, with a view to restore soundness or health; as in amputation, lithotomy and the like.
To act; to have agency; to produce any effect.
To effect; to produce by agency. The same cause would operate a diminution of the value of stock
operatical: Pertaining to the opera; a word used by musicians.
operating: Acting; exerting agency or power; performing some manual act in surgery.
operation: The act or process of operating; agency; the exertion of power, physical, mechanical or moral. Speculative painting without the assistance of manual operation, can never attain to perfection. The pain and sickness caused by manna are the effects of its operation on the stomach. So we speak of the operation of motives, reasons or arguments on the mind, the operation of causes.
Action; effect. Many medicinal drugs of rare operation.
Process; manipulation; series of acts in experiments; as in chimistry or metallurgy.
In surgery, any methodical action of the hand, or of the hand with instruments, on the human body, with a view to heal a part diseased, fractured or dislocated, as in amputation.
Action or movements of an army or fleet; as military or naval operations.
operative: Having the power of acting; exerting force, physical or moral; having or exerting agency; active in the production of effects. In actions of religion we should be zealous, active and operative, so far as prudence will permit. It holds in all operative principles, especially in morality.
operator: He or that which operates; he or that which produces an effect.
In surgery, the person who performs some act upon the human body by means of the hand, or with instruments; as a skillful operator.
operculate: 2. In botany, having a lid or cover, as a capsule.
operculated: 2. In botany, having a lid or cover, as a capsule.
operculiform: Having the form of a lid or cover.
opetide: The ancient time of marriage, from Epiphany to Ash Wednesday.
ophidian: Pertaining to serpents; designating an order of vertebral animals destitute of feet or fins.
ophidion: a fish of the anguilliform kind, resembling the common eel, but shorter, more depressed and of a paler color; found in the mediterranean.
ophiologist: One versed in the natural history of serpents.
ophiology: That part of natural history which treats of serpents, or which arranges and describes the several kinds.
ophiomancy: In antiquity, the art of divining or predicting events by serpents, as by their manner of eating or by their coils.
ophiomorphous: Having the form of a serpent.
ophite: Green porphyry, or serpentine; a variety of greenstone of a dusky green color of different shades, sprinkled with spots of a lighter green; in other words, containing greenish white crystals of feldspar.
ophiuchus: A constellation in the northern hemisphere.
ophthalmoscopy: A branch of physiognomy which deduces the knowledge of a man’s temper and manner from the appearance of the eyes.
ophthalmy: A disease of the eyes; an inflammation of the membranes which invest the eye. Inflammation of the eye or its appendages.
opiate: Primarily, a medicine of a thicker consistence than syrup, prepared with opium. A soft electuary. Electuaries when soft are call opiata. But in modern usage generally,
Any medicine that has the quality of inducing sleep or repose; a narcotic.
That which induces rest or inaction; that which quiets uneasiness. They chose atheism as an opiate.
opinator: One fond of his own opinions; one who holds an opinion.
opiner: One who thinks or holds an opinion. Obs.
opiniaster: Unduly attached to one’s own opinion, or stiff in adhering to it. Obs.
opiniastrous: Unduly attached to one’s own opinion, or stiff in adhering to it. Obs.
opiniatre: Unduly attached to one’s own opinion, or stiff in adhering to it. Obs.
opiniate: To maintain one’s opinion with obstinacy. Obs.
opiniated: Unduly attached to one’s own opinions.
opiniative: Very stiff in adherence to preconceived notions.
opiniator: One unduly attached to his own opinion. Obs.
opiniatry: Unreasonable attachment to one’s own notions; obstinacy in opinions. Obs.
opinion: The judgment which the mind forms of any proposition, statement, theory or event, the truth or falsehood of which is supported by a degree of evidence that renders it probably, but does not produce absolute knowledge or certainty. It has been a received opinion that all matter is comprised in four elements. This opinion is proved by many discoveries to be false. From circumstances we form opinions respecting future events. Opinion is when the assent of the understanding is so far gained by evidence of probability, that it rather inclines to one persuasion than to another, yet not without a mixture of uncertainty or doubting.

The judgment or sentiments which the mind forms of persons or their qualities. We speak of a good opinion, a favorable opinion, a bad opinion, a private opinion, and public or general opinion. Friendship gives a man a peculiar right and claim to the good opinion of his friend.
Settled judgment or persuasion; as religious opinions; political opinion.
Favorable judgment; estimation. In actions of arms, small matters are of great moment, especially when they serve to raise an opinion of commanders. However, I have no opinion of these things - .
opinionate: a2. Stiff in opinion; firmly or unduly adhering to one’s own opinion; obstinate in opinion.
opinionated: a2. Stiff in opinion; firmly or unduly adhering to one’s own opinion; obstinate in opinion.
opinionative: Fond of preconceived notions; unduly attached to one’s own opinions.
opinionatively: With undue fondness for one’s own opinions; stubbornly.
opinionativeness: Excessive attachment to one’s own opinions; obstinacy in opinion.
opinionist: One fond of his own notions, or one unduly attached to his own opinions.
opisthodome: In Greece, a part or place in the back part of a house.
opium: Opium is the inspissated juice of the capsules of the papaver somniferum, or somniferous white poppy with which the fields in Asia Minor are sown, as ours are with wheat and rye. It flows from incisions made in the heads of the plant, and the best flows from the first incision. It is imported into Europe and America from the Levant and the East Indies. It is brought in cakes or masses weighing from eight ounces to a pound. It is heavy, of a dense texture, of a brownish yellow color, not perfectly dry, but easily receiving an impression from the finger; it has a dead and faint smell, and its taste is bitter and acrid. Opium is of great use as a medicine.
opobalsam: The balm or balsam of Gilead. It has a yellowish or greenish yellow color, a warm bitterish aromatic taste, and an acidulous fragrant smell. It is held in esteem as a medicine and as an odoriferous unguent and cosmetic. The shrub or tree producing this balsam is of the genus Amyris, and grows spontaneously in Arabia Felix.
opodeldoc: The name of a plaster, said to have been invented by Mindererus; but in modern usage.
A saponaceous camphorated liniment; a solution of soap in ardent spirits, with the addition of camphor and essential oils.
opopanax: A gum-resin of a tolerably firm texture, brought in loose granules or drops, sometimes in larger masses. This substance on the outside is of a brownish red color, with specks of white, and within of a dusky yellow or whitish color. It has a strong smell and an acrid taste. It is obtained from the roots of an umbelliferous plant of the genus Pastinaca or parsnep, and is brought from Turkey and the East Indies.
opossum: A quadruped of the genus Didelphis. It has a prehensile tail, like some of the monkeys, and is distinguished by a pouch or false belly, in which it protects and carries its young. The name is also given to other species of the genus, some of which want the pouch.
oppidan: An appellation given to the students of Eton school in England.
oppilate: To crown together; to fill with obstructions.
oppilation: The act of filling or crowding together; a stopping by redundant matter; obstructions, particularly in the lower intestines.
opponency: The opening of an academical disputation; the proposition of objections to a tenet; an exercise for a degree.
opponent: One that opposes; particularly, one that opposes in controversy, disputation or argument. It is sometimes applied to the person that begins a dispute by raising objections to a tenet or doctrine, and is correlative to defendant or respondent. In common usage, however, it is applicable to either party in a controversy, denoting any person who opposes another or his cause. Opponent may sometimes be used for adversary, and for antagonist, but not with strict propriety, as the word does not necessarily imply enmity nor bodily strife. Nor is it well used in the sense of rival or competitor.
opportune: Properly, having come or being present at a proper time; hence, seasonable; timely; well timed. It agrees with seasonable rather than with convenient, though the sense of the latter may be included in it . Perhaps in view of those bright confines, whence with neighboring arms, and opportune excursion, we may chance re-enter heaven.
opportunely: Seasonably; at a time favorable for the purpose. It has been applied to place, as well as to time, but its proper application is to time, and hence it accords with seasonably, rather than with conveniently.
opportunity: Fit or convenient time; a time favorable for the purpose; suitable time combined with other favorable circumstances. Suitableness of time is the predominant signification, but it includes generally circumstances of place and other conveniences adapted to the end desired. A wise man will make more opportunities than he finds. I had an opportunity to see the cloud descent. Neglect no opportunity of doing good.
Convenient means. I had an opportunity of sending the letter, or no opportunity to send it. Opportunities rarely occur on frequently offer.
oppose: To set; against; to put in opposition, with a view to counterbalance or countervail, and thus to hinder defeat, destroy or prevent effect; as, to oppose one argument to another. I may without presumption oppose my single opinion to his.

To act against; to resist, either by physical means, by arguments or other means. The army opposed the progress of the enemy, but without success. Several members of the house strenuously opposed the bill, but it passed.
To check; to resist effectually. The army was not able to oppose the progress of the enemy.
To place in front; to set opposite.
opposed: To act adversely; with against; as, a servant opposed against the act.
To object or act against in controversy.
Set in opposition; resisted. 2.a. Being in opposition in principle or in act; adverse. Certain characters were formerly opposed to it.
opposer: One that opposes; an opponent in party, in principle, in controversy or argument. We speak of the opposers of public measures; the opposers of ecclesiastical discipline; an opposer of christianity or of orthodoxy.
One who acts in opposition; one who resists; as an opposer of law or of the execution of law.
An antagonist; an adversary; an enemy; a rival.
opposite: Standing or situated in front; facing; as an edifice opposite to the Exchange. Brooklyn lies opposite to New York, or on the opposite side of the river.
Adverse; repugnant. - Novels, by which the reader is misled into another sort of pleasure opposite to that designed in an epic poem.
Contrary; as words of opposite significations; opposite terms. The medicine had an effect opposite to what was expected.
In botany, growing in pairs, each pair decussated or crossing that above and below it; as opposite leaves or branches.
An opponent; an adversary; an enemy; an antagonist.
oppositely: In front; in a situation to face each other.
Adversely; against each other. Winds from all quarters oppositely blow.
oppositeness: The state of being opposite or contrary.
oppositifolious: In botany, opposite to the leaf; as an oppositifolious peduncle.
opposition: Situation so as to front something else; a standing over against; as the opposition of two mountains or buildings.
The act of opposing; attempt to check, restrain or defeat. he makes opposition to the measure; the bill passed without opposition. Will any opposition be made to the suit, to the claim or demand?
Obstacle. the river meets with no opposition in its course to the ocean.
Resistance; as the opposition of enemies. Virtue will break through all opposition.
Contrariety; repugnance in principle; as the opposition of the heart to the laws of God.
oppositionist: One that belongs to the party opposing the administration.
oppositive: that may be put in opposition.
oppress: To load or burden with unreasonable impositions; to treat with unjust severity, rigor or hardship; as, to oppress a nation with taxes or contributions; to oppress one by compelling him to perform unreasonable service.
To overpower; to overburden; as, to be oppressed with grief.
to sit or lie heavy on; as, excess of good oppresses the stomach.
oppressed: burdened with unreasonable impositions; overpowered; overburdened; depressed.
oppression: The act of oppressing; the imposition of unreasonable burdens, either in taxes or services; cruelty; severity.:
The state of being oppressed or overburdened; misery. The Lord - saw the oppression of Israel.2 Kings 13:4.
Depression; dullness of spirits; lassitude of body.
A sense of heaviness or weight in the breast.
oppressive: Unreasonably burdensome; unjustly severe; as oppressive taxes; oppressive exactions of service.
Heavy; overpowering; overwhelming; as oppressive grief or woe.
oppressively: In a manner to oppress; with unreasonable severity.
oppressor: One that oppresses; one that imposes unjust burdens on others; one that harasses others with unjust laws or unreasonable severity. Power when employed to relieve the oppressed and to punish the oppressor, becomes a great blessing.
opprobrious: Reproachful and contemptuous; scurrilous; as opprobrious language; opprobrious words or terms.
Blasted with infamy; despised; rendered hateful; as an opprobrious name.
opprobriously: With reproach mingled with contempt; scurrilously.
opprobrium: Reproach mingled with contempt or disdain.
oppugn: oppu’ne. To attack; to oppose; to resist. They said the manner of their impeachment they could not but conceive did oppugn the rights of parliament.
oppugner: oppu’ner. One who opposes or attacks; that which opposes.
opsimathy: Late education; education late in life.
opsonation: A catering; a buying of provisions.
optation: A desiring; the expression of a wish.
optative: Expressing desire or wish. The optative mode, in grammar, is that form of the verb in which wish or desire is expressed.
optic: Relating or pertaining to vision or sight.

Relating to the science of optics. Optic angle, is that which the optic axes of the eyes make with one another, as they tend to meet at some distance before the eyes. Optic axis, is the axis of the eye, or a line going through the middle of the pupil and the center of the eye.
optical: Relating or pertaining to vision or sight.

Relating to the science of optics. Optic angle, is that which the optic axes of the eyes make with one another, as they tend to meet at some distance before the eyes. Optic axis, is the axis of the eye, or a line going through the middle of the pupil and the center of the eye.
optician: Aperson skilled in the science of optics.
One who makes or sells optic glasses and instruments.
optics: The science which treats of light and the phenomena of vision.
optimacy: The body of nobles; the nobility.
optimism: The opinion or doctrine that every thing in nature is ordered for the best; or the order of things in the universe that is adapted to produce the most good. The true and amiable philosophy of optimism. A system of strict optimism may be the real system in both cases.
option: The power of choosing; the right of choice or election; as the archbishop’s option in collating to a vacant benefice. There is an option left to the United States of America, whether they will be respectable and prosperous, or contemptible and miserable, as a nation.
Choice; election; preference. He ought not to complain of his lot; it was his own option. We leave this to your own option.
optional: Left to one’s wish or choice; depending on choice or preference. It is optional with you to go or stay.
Leaving something to choice. Original writs are either optional or peremptory.
opulent: Wealthy; rich; affluent; having a large estate or property.
or: a termination of Latin nouns, is a contraction of vir, a man, or from the same radix. The same word vir, is in our mother tongue, wer, and from this we have the English termination er. It denotes an agent, as in actorWe annex it to many words of English origin, as in lessor, as we do er to words of Latin and Greek origin, as in astronomerIn general, or is annexed to words of Latin, and er to those of English origin.

A connective that marks an alternative. “You may read or may write;” that is, you may do one of the things at your pleasure, but not both. It corresponds to either. You may either ride to London, or to Windsor. It often connects a series of words or propositions, presenting a choice of either. He may study law or medicine or divinity, or he may enter into trade. Or sometimes begins a sentence, but in this case it expresses an alternative with the foregoing sentence. Matthew 7:4, 9, 10 and Matthew 9:5. In poetry, or is sometimes used for either. For thy vast bounties are so numberless, that them or to conceal or else to tell is equally impossible. Or is often used to express an alternative of terms, definitions or explanations of the same thing in different words. Thus we say, a thing is a square, or a figure under four equal sides and angles. Or ever. In this phrase, or is supposed to be
orach: A plant of the genus Atriplex, used as a substitute for spinage. Wild orach is of the genus Chenopodium.
orrach: A plant of the genus Atriplex, used as a substitute for spinage. Wild orach is of the genus Chenopodium.
oracle: Among pagans, the answer of a god or some person reputed to be a god, to an inquiry made respecting some affair of importance, usually respecting some future event, as the success of an enterprise or battle.
The deity who gave or was supposed to give answers to inquiries; as the Delphic oracle.
The place where the answers were given.

Among christians, oracles, in the plural, denotes the communications, revelations or messages delivered by God to prophets. In this sense it is rarely used in the singular; but we say, the oracles of God, divine oracles, meaning the Scriptures.
The sanctuary or most holy place in the temple, in which was deposited the ark of the covenant.7 Kings 6:5-31.
oracular: Uttering oracles; as an oracular tongue. The oraculous seer.
Grace; venerable; like an oracle; as an oracular shade. They have something venerable and oracular in that unadorned gravity and shortness in the expression.
Positive; authoritative; magisterial; as oraculous expressions of sentiments.
Obscure; ambiguous, like the oracles of pagan deities.
oraculous: Uttering oracles; as an oracular tongue. The oraculous seer.
Grace; venerable; like an oracle; as an oracular shade. They have something venerable and oracular in that unadorned gravity and shortness in the expression.
Positive; authoritative; magisterial; as oraculous expressions of sentiments.
Obscure; ambiguous, like the oracles of pagan deities.
oracularly: In the manner of an oracle.
oraculously: In the manner of an oracle.
oraison: Prayer; verbal supplication or oral worship; now written orison.
oral: Uttered by the mouth or in words; spoken, not written; as oral traditions; oral testimony; oral law.
orally: By mouth; in words, without writing; as traditions derived orally from ancestors.
orange: The fruit of a species of Citrus which grows in warm climates. The fruit is round and depressed; it has a rough rind, which when ripe is yellow. This contains a vesicular pulp inclosed in nine cells for seeds. The tree producing oranges grows to the height of ten or twelve feet and bears the same name.' .
orange-peel: The rind of an orange separated from the fruit.
orange-tawny: Of the color of an orange.
orang-outang: The satyr or great ape (Simia satyrus,) an animal with a flat face and deformed resemblance of the human form. These animals walk erect like man, feed on fruits, sleep on trees, and make a shelter against inclemencies of the weather. They grow to the height of six feet, are remarkable strong, and wield weapons with the hand. They are solitary animals, inhabiting the interior of Africa and the isles of Sumatra, Borneo and Java. The orang-outang is found only in S. Eastern Asia. The African animal resembling it, is the chimpanzee (Simia troglodytes. ) .
oration: A speech or discourse composed according to the rules of oratory, and spoken in public. Orations may be reduced to three kinds; demonstrative, deliberative, and judicial.

In modern usage, an oration differs from a sermon, from an argument at the bar, and from a speech before a deliberative assembly. The word is now applied chiefly to discourses pronounced on special occasions, as a funeral oration, an oration on some anniversaryand to academic declamations.
A harangue; a public speech or address.
orator: A public speaker. In ancient Rome, orators were advocates for clients in the forum and before the senate and people. They were employed in causes of importance instead of the common patron.
In modern usage, a person who pronounces a discourse publicly on some special occasion, as on the celebration of some memorable event.
An eloquent public speaker; a speaker, by way of eminence. We say, a man writes and reasons well, but is no orator. Lord Chatham was an orator.
In France, a speaker in debate in a legislative body.
oratorial: Pertaining to an orator or to oratory; rhetorical; becoming an orator. We say, a man has many oratorical flourishes, or he speaks in an oratorical way.
oratorical: Pertaining to an orator or to oratory; rhetorical; becoming an orator. We say, a man has many oratorical flourishes, or he speaks in an oratorical way.
oratorio: In Italian music, a sacred drama of dialogues, containing recitatives, duets, trios, ritornellos, chorusesThe subjects are mostly taken from the Scriptures.
A place of worship; a chapel.
oratory: The art of speaking well, or of speaking according to the rules of rhetoric, in order to persuade. To constitute oratory, the speaking must be just and pertinent to the subject; it must be methodical, all parts of the discourse being disposed in due order and connection; and it must be embellished with the beauties of language and pronounced with eloquence. Oratory consists of four parts, invention, disposition, elocution, and pronunciation.
Among the Romanists, a close apartment near a bed-chamber, furnished with an altar, a crucifixfor private devotions.
A place allotted for prayer, or a place for public worship.
orb: A spherical body; as the celestial orbs.
In astronomy, a hollow globe or sphere.
A wheel; a circular body that revolves or rolls; as the orbs of a chariot.
A circle; a sphere defined by a line; as, he move in a larger orb.
A circle described by any mundane sphere; an orbit.
orbation: Privation of parents or children, or privation in general.
orbed: Formed into a circle or round shape.
Rounded or covered on the exterior. The wheels were orbed with gold.
orbicular: Spherical; circular; in the form of an orb.
orbicularness: Sphericity; the state of being orbicular.
orbiculate: Made or being in the form of an orb. In botany, an orbiculate or orbicular leaf is one that has the periphery of a circle, or both its longitudinal and transverse diameters equal.
orbiculated: Made or being in the form of an orb. In botany, an orbiculate or orbicular leaf is one that has the periphery of a circle, or both its longitudinal and transverse diameters equal.
orbiculation: The state of being made in the form of an orb.
orbis: A fish of a circular form. It is covered with a firm hard skin full of small prickles, but is destitute of scales. It is unfit for food.
orb-fish: A fish of a circular form. It is covered with a firm hard skin full of small prickles, but is destitute of scales. It is unfit for food.
orbit: In astronomy, the path of a planet or comet; the curve line which a planet describes in its periodical revolution round its central body; as the orbit of Jupiter or Mercury. The orbit of the earth is nearly one hundred and ninety millions of miles in diameter. The orbit of the moon is 480,000 miles in diameter. The orbits of the planets are elliptical.
In anatomy, the cavity in which the eye is situated.
orbitude: Bereavement by loss of parents or children.
orbity: Bereavement by loss of parents or children.
orc: A sea-fish, a species of whale. The Delphinus orca is the grampus.
orchard: An inclosure for fruit trees. In Great Britain, a department of the garden appropriated to fruit trees of all kinds, but chiefly to apple trees. In America, any piece of land set with apple trees, is called an orchard; and orchards are usually cultivated land, being either grounds for mowing or tillage. In some parts of the country, a piece of ground planted with peach trees is called a peach orchard. But in most cases, I believe the orchard in both countries is distinct from the garden.
orchester: The part of a theater or other public place appropriated to the musicians. In the Grecian theaters, the orchester was a part of the stage; it was of a semicircular form and surrounded with seats. In the Roman theaters, it was no part of the scena, but answered nearly to the pit in modern play houses, and was occupied by senators and other persons of distinction.
The body of performers in the orchester.
orchestra: The part of a theater or other public place appropriated to the musicians. In the Grecian theaters, the orchester was a part of the stage; it was of a semicircular form and surrounded with seats. In the Roman theaters, it was no part of the scena, but answered nearly to the pit in modern play houses, and was occupied by senators and other persons of distinction.
The body of performers in the orchester.
orchestral: Pertaining to an orchester; suitable for or performed in the orchester.
orchis: A genus of plants, called fool-stones.
ord: An edge or point; as in ordhelm. Ord signifies beginning; as in ords and ends.
ordain: Properly, to set; to establish in a particular office or order; hence, to invest with a ministerial function or sacerdotal power; to introduce and establish or settle in the pastoral office with the customary forms and solemnities; as, to ordain a minister of the gospel. In America, men are ordained over a particular church and congregation, or as evangelists without the charge of a particular church, or as deacons in the episcopal church.
To appoint; to decree. Jeroboam ordained a feast in the eighth month.7 Kings 12:32. As many as were ordained to eternal lifeAcis 13:48.
To set; to establish; to institute; to constitute. Mulmutius ordained our laws.
To set apart for an office; to appoint. Jesus ordained twelve that they should be with him. Mark 3:14.
To appoint; to prepare. For Tophet is ordained of old. Isaiah 30:33.
ordained: Appointed; instituted; established; invested with ministerial or pastoral functions; settled.
ordainer: One who ordains, appoints or invests with sacerdotal powers.
ordaining: Appointing; establishing; investing with sacerdotal or pastoral functions.
ordeal: An ancient form of trial to determine guilt or innocence, practiced by the rude nations of Europe, and still practiced in the East Indies. In England, the ordeal was of two sorts, fire-ordeal and water- ordeal; the former being confined to persons of higher rank, the latter to the common people. Both might be performed by deputy, but the principal was to answer for the success of the trial. Fire-ordeal was performed either by taking in the hand a piece of red hot iron, or by walking barefoot and blindfold over nine red hot plowshares laid lengthwise at unequal distances; and if the person escapes unhurt, he was adjudged innocent, otherwise he was condemned as guilty. Water-ordeal was performed, either by plunging the bare arm to the elbow in boiling water, or by casting the person suspected into a river or pond of cold water and if he floated without an effort to swim, it was an evidence
order: Regular disposition or methodical arrangement of things; a word of extensive application; as the order of troops or parade; the order of books in a library; the order of proceedings in a legislative assembly. Order is the life of business. Good order is the foundation of all good things.
Proper state; as the muskets are all in good order. When the bodily organs are in order, a person is in health; when they are out of order, he is indisposed.
Adherence to the point in discussion, according to established rules of debate; as, the member is not in order, that is, he wanders from the question.
Established mode of proceeding. The motion is not in order.
Regularity; settled mode of operation. This fact could not occur in the order of nature; it is against the natural order of things.&
orderless: Without regularity; disorderly; out of rule.
orderliness: Regularity; a state of being methodical.
orderly: Well regulated; performed in good order; not tumultuous; as an orderly march.

Not unruly; not inclined to break from inclosures; peaceable. We say, cattle are orderly. Orderly book, in military affairs, a book for every company, in which the sergeants write general and regimental orders. Orderly sergeant, a military officer who attends on a superior officer.
ordinal: Noting order; as the ordinal numbers, first, second, third
A book containing the order of divine service; a ritual.
ordinance: A rule established by authority; a permanent rule of action. An ordinance may be a law or statute of sovereign power. In this sense it is often used in the Scriptures. Exodus 15:25; Numbers 10:8; Ezra 3:10. It may also signify a decree, edict or rescript, and the word has sometimes been applied to the statutes of Parliament, but these are usually called acts or laws. In the United States, it is never applied to the acts of Congress, or of a state legislature.
Established rite or ceremony. Hebrews 9:7, 70. In this sense, baptism and the Lord’s supper are denominated ordinances.
ordinarily: Primarily, according to established rules or settled method; hence, commonly; usually; in most cases as a winter more than ordinarily severe.
ordinary: According to established order; methodical; regular; customary; as the ordinary forms of law or justice.
Common; usual. Method is not less requisite in ordinary conversation than in writing.
Of common rank; not distinguished by superior excellence; as an ordinary reader; men of ordinary judgment.
Plain; not handsome; as an ordinary woman; a person of an ordinary form; an ordinary face.
ordinate: Regular; methodical. An ordinate figure is one whose sides and angles are equal.

In geometry and conic sections, a line drawn from any point of the circumference of an ellipsis or other conic section, perpendicularly across the axis to the other side. An ordinate is a line drawn perpendicular to the axis of a curve and terminating the curvilinear space. Ordinates of a curve, right lines parallel to one another, terminated by the curve, and bisected by a right line called the diameter.
ordination: The state of being ordained or appointed; established order or tendency consequent on a decree. Virtue and vice have a natural ordination to the happiness and misery of life respectively.
The act of conferring holy orders or sacerdotal power; called also consecration.

In the presbyterian and congregational churches, the act of settling or establishing a licensed cleryman over a church and congregation with pastoral charge and authority; also, the act of conferring on a clergyman the powers of a settled minister of the gospel, without the charge or oversight of a particular church, but with the general powers of an evangelist, who is authorized to form churches and administer the sacraments of baptism and the Lord’s supper, wherever he may be called to officiate.
ordnance: Cannon or great guns, mortars and howitzers; artillery.
ordonnance: In painting, the disposition of the parts of a picture, either in regard to the whole piece or to the several parts.
ore: The compound of a metal and some other substance, as oxygen, sulphur or carbon, called its mineralizer, by which its properties are disguised or lost. Metals found free from such combination and exhibiting naturally their appropriate character, are not called ores, but native metals.
orfgild: The restitution of goods or money stolen, if taken in the day time.
orgal: Argal; lees of wine dried; tartar.
organ: A natural instrument of action or operation, or by which some process is carried on. Thus the arteries and veins of animal bodies are organs of circulation; the lungs are organs of respiration; the nerves are organs of perception and sensation; the muscles are organs of motion; the ears are organs of hearing; the tongue is the organ of speech.
The instrument or means of conveyance or communication. A secretary of state is the organ of communication between the government and a foreign power.
The largest and most harmonious of wind instruments of music, consisting of pipes which are filled with wind, and stops touched by the fingers. It is blown by a bellows.
organ-builder: An artist whose occupation is to construct organs.
organic: Pertaining to an organ or to organs; consisting of organs or containing them; as the organic structure of the human body or of plants.
Produced by the organs; as organic pleasure.

Instrumental; acting as instruments of nature or art to a certain end; as organic arts. Organic bodies, are such as possess organs, on the action of which depend their growth and perfection; as animals and plants.
organical: Pertaining to an organ or to organs; consisting of organs or containing them; as the organic structure of the human body or of plants.
Produced by the organs; as organic pleasure.

Instrumental; acting as instruments of nature or art to a certain end; as organic arts. Organic bodies, are such as possess organs, on the action of which depend their growth and perfection; as animals and plants.
organically: With organs; with organical structure or disposition of parts. The bodies of animals and plants are organically framed.
organism: Organical structure; as the organism of bodies.
organist: One who plays on the organ.
One who sung in parts; an old musical use of the word.
organization: The act or process of forming organs or instruments of action.

The act of forming or arranging the parts of a compound or complex body in a suitable manner for use or service; the act of distributing into suitable divisions and appointing the proper officers, as an army or a government. The first organization of the general government.
Structure; form; suitable disposition of parts which are to act together in a compound body.
organize: To form with suitable organs; to construct so that one part may cooperate with another. Those nobler faculties of the soul organized matter could never produce.
To sing in parts; as, to organize the halleluiah.

To distribute into suitable parts and appoint proper officers, that the whole may act as one body; as, to organize an army. So we say, to organize the house of representatives, which is done by the appointment of officers and verification of the powers of the several members. So we Say, a club, a party or a faction is organized, when it takes a systemized form. This original and supreme will organizes the government.
organized: Formed with organs; constructed organically; systemized; reduced to a form in which all the parts may act together to one end. Animals and plants are organized bodies. Minerals are not organized bodies.
organizing: Constructing with suitable organs; reducing to system in order to produce united action to one end.
organ-loft: The loft where an organ stands.
organography: In botany, a description of the organs of plants, or of the names and kinds of their organs.
organ-pipe: The pipe of a musical organ.
organ-stop: The stop of an organ, or any collection of pipes under one general name.
organzine: Silk twisted into threads; thrown silk.
orgasm: Immoderate excitement or action; as the orgasm of the blood or spirits.
orgeat: A liquor extracted from barley and sweet almonds.
orgeis: A fish, called also organ-ling; supposed to be from Orkneys, on the coast of which it is taken.
orgies: Frantic revels at the feast in honor of Bacchus, or the feast itself. This feast was held in the night; hence nocturnal orgies.
orgues: In the military art, long thick pieces of timber, pointed and shod with iron and hung over a gateway, to be let down in case of attack.
Amachine composed of several musket barrels united, by means of which several explosions are made at once to defend breaches.
orichalch: A metallic substance resembling gold in color, but inferior in value; the brass of the ancients.
orichalcum: A metallic substance resembling gold in color, but inferior in value; the brass of the ancients.
oriel: A small apartment next a hall, where particular persons dine; a sort of recess. Obs.
oriol: A small apartment next a hall, where particular persons dine; a sort of recess. Obs.
orient: Rising, as the sun. - Moon, that now meet’st the orient sun. The orient morn.
Bright; shining; glittering; as orient pearls.
The east; the part of the horizon where the sun first appears in the morning.
oriental: Eastern; situated in the east; as oriental seas or countries.
Proceeding from the east; as the oriental radiations of the sun.
A native or inhabitant of some eastern part of the world. We give the appellation to the inhabitants of Asia from the Hellespont and Mediterranean to Japan.
orientalism: An eastern mode of speech; an idiom of the eastern languages.
orientalist: An inhabitant of the eastern parts of the world.
One versed in the eastern languages and literature.
orientality: The state of being oriental or eastern.
orifice: The mouth or aperture of a tube, pipe or other cavity; as the orifice of an artery or vein; the orifice of a wound. The orifice of Etna.
oriflamb: The ancient royal standard of France.
origan: Marjoram, a genus of plants. One species of this genus is a rich aromatic, excellent for culinary purposes.
origanum: Marjoram, a genus of plants. One species of this genus is a rich aromatic, excellent for culinary purposes.
origenism: The doctrines or tenets of Origen, who united Platonism with christianity.
origenist: A follower of Origen of Alexandria, a celebrated christian father. The Origenists held that the souls of men have a pre-existent state; that they are holy intelligences, and sin before they are united to the body; that Christ will be crucified hereafter for the salvation of devils
origin: The first existence or beginning of any thing; as the origin of Rome. In history it is necessary, if practicable, to trace all events to their origin.

Fountain; source; cause; that from which any thing primarily proceeds; that which gives existence or beginning. The apostasy is believed to have been the origin of moral evil. The origin of many of our customs is lost in antiquity. Nations, like individuals, are ambitious to trace their descent from an honorable origin.
original: Fountain; source; cause; that from which any thing primarily proceeds; that which gives existence or beginning. The apostasy is believed to have been the origin of moral evil. The origin of many of our customs is lost in antiquity. Nations, like individuals, are ambitious to trace their descent from an honorable origin.
First in order; preceding all others; as the original state of man; the original laws of a country; original rights or powers; the original question in debate.

Primitive; pristine; as the original perfection of Adam. Original sin, as applied to Adam, was his first act of disobedience in eating the forbidden fruit; as applied to his posterity, it is understood to mean either the sin of Adam imputed to his posterity, or that corruption of nature, or total depravity, which has been derived from him in consequence of his apostasy. On this subject divines are not agreed. In strictness, original sin is an improper use of words, as sin, ex vi termini, implies volition and the transgression of a known rule of duty by a moral agent. But this application of the words has been established by long use, and it serves to express ideas which many wise and good men entertain on this subject.
Having the power to originate new thoughts or combinations of thought; as an original genius.
originality: The quality or state of being original.
The power of originating or producing new thoughts, or uncommon combinations of thought; as originality of genius.
originally: Primarily; from the beginning or origin. God is originally holy in himself.
By the first author; as a book originally written by another hand.
originalness: The quality or state of being original.
originary: Productive; causing existence. The production of animals in the originary way, requires a certain degree of warmth.
originate: To cause to be; to bring into existence; to produce what is new. The change is to be effected without a decomposition of the whole civil and political mass, for the purpose of originating a new civil order out of the elements of society. That matter which cannot think, will, or originate motion, should communicate thought, volition and motivity, is plainly impossible.
To take first existence; to have origin; to be begun. The scheme originated with the governor and council. It originated in pure benevolence.
origination: The act of bringing or coming into existence; first production. Descartes first introduced the fancy of making a world, and deducing the origination of the universe from mechanical principles.
Mode of production or bringing into being. This eruca is propagated by animal parents, to wit, butterflies, after the common origination of all caterpillars.
orillon: In fortification, a rounding of earth, faced with a wall, raised on the shoulder of those bastions that have casemates, to cover the cannon in the retired flank, and prevent their being dismounted.
oriole: A genus of birds of the order of picae.
orion: A constellation in the southern hemisphere, containing seventy eight stars.
orison: A prayer of supplication. Lowly they bowed adoring, and began their orisons, each morning duly paid.
orle: In heraldry, an ordinary in the form of a fillet, round the shield.
orlet: In architecture, a fillet under the ovolo of a capital.
orlo: In architecture, a fillet under the ovolo of a capital.
orlop: In a ship of war, a platform of planks laid over the beams in the hold, on which the cables are usually coiled. It contains also sail- rooms, carpenters’ cabins and other apartments. Also, a tier of beams below the lower deck for a like purpose.
ornament: That which embellishes; something which, added to another thing, renders it more beautiful to the eye. The chains, and the bracelets, and the mufflers, the bonnets and the ornaments of the legs - Isaiah 3:19-20.
In architecture, ornaments are sculpture or carved work.
Embellishment; decoration; additional beauty. - The ornament of a meek and quiet spirit which is in the sight of God of great price. 7 Peter 3:4.
To adorn; to deck; to embellish.
ornamental: Serving to decorate; giving additional beauty; embellishing. Some think it most ornamental to wear their bracelets on their wrists; others about their ankles.
ornamentally: In such a manner as to add embellishment.
orniscopics: Divination by the observation of fowls.
orniscopist: One who views the flight of fowls in order to foretell future events by their manner of flight.
ornithologist: A person who is skilled in the natural history of fowls, who understands their form, structure, habits and uses; one who describes birds.
ornithology: The science of fowls, which comprises a knowledge of their form, structure, habits and uses.
ornithomancy: Augury, a species of divination by means of fowls, their flight.
orological: Pertaining to a description of mountains.
orology: The science or description of mountains.
orphan: A child who is bereaved of father or mother or of both.
orphean: Pertaining to Orpheus, the poet and musician; as Orphic hymns.
orphic: Pertaining to Orpheus, the poet and musician; as Orphic hymns.
orpheus: A fish found in the Mediterranean, broad, flat and thick, and sometimes weighing twenty pounds. The orpheus of the Greeks is said to have been a different fish.
orpiment: Sulphuret of arsenic, found native and then an ore of arsenic, or artificially composed. The native orpiment appears in yellow, brilliant and seemingly talcky masses of various sizes. The red orpiment is called realgar. It is more or less lively and transparent, and often crystallized in bright needles. In this form it is called ruby of arsenic.
orpine: A plant of the genus Sedum, lesser houseleek or live-long. The bastard orpine is of the genus Andrachine; the lesser orpine of the genus Crassula.
orrery: A machine so constructed as to represent by the movements of its parts, the motions and phases of the planets in their orbits. This machine was invented by George Graham, but Rowley, a workman, borrowed one from him, and made a copy for the earl of Orrery, after whom it was named by Sir Richard Steele. similar machines are called also planetariums.
orris: The plant iris, of which orris seems to be a corruption; fleur de lis or flag-flower.
A sort of gold or silver lace.
ortalon: A small bird of the genus Alauda.
orthite: A mineral occurring in straight layers in felspath rock with albiteIt is of a blackish brown color, resembling gadolinite, but differs from it is fusibility.
orthoceratite: The name of certain fossil univalve shells, straight or but slightly curved, arranged by Cuvier in the genus’ Nantilus .
orthodoxness: The state of being sound in the faith, or of according with the doctrines of Scripture.
orthodoxy: Soundness of faith; a belief in the genuine doctrines taught in the Scriptures. Basil bears full and clear testimony to Gregory’s orthodoxy.
Consonance to genuine scriptural doctrines; as the orthodoxy of a creed.
orthodromics: The art of sailing in the arc of a great circle, which is the shortest distance between any two points on the surface of the globe.
orthodromy: The sailing in a straight course.
orthoepist: One who pronounces words correctly, or who is well skilled in pronunciation.
orthoepy: The art of uttering words with propriety; a correct pronunciation of words.
orthographer: One that spells words correctly, according to common usage.
orthographic: Correctly spelled; written with the proper letters.

Pertaining to the spelling of words; as, to make an orthographical mistake. Orthographic projection of the sphere, a delineation of the sphere upon a plane that cuts it in the middle, the eye being supposed to be placed at an infinite distance from it. A projection in which the eye is supposed to be at an infinite distance; so called because the perpendiculars from any point of the sphere will all fall in the common intersection of the sphere with the plane of the projection.
orthographical: Correctly spelled; written with the proper letters.

Pertaining to the spelling of words; as, to make an orthographical mistake. Orthographic projection of the sphere, a delineation of the sphere upon a plane that cuts it in the middle, the eye being supposed to be placed at an infinite distance from it. A projection in which the eye is supposed to be at an infinite distance; so called because the perpendiculars from any point of the sphere will all fall in the common intersection of the sphere with the plane of the projection.
orthographically: According to the rules’ of proper spelling.
In the manner’ of = orthographic projection.
orthography: The art of writing words with the proper letters, according to common usage.
The part of grammar which treats of the nature and properties of letters, and of the art of writing words correctly.
The practice of spelling or writing words with the proper letters.

In geometry, the art of delineating the fore right plane or side of any object, and of expressing the elevations of each part; so called because it determines things by perpendicular lines falling on the geometrical plane.
In architecture, the elevation of a building, showing all the parts in their true proportion.
orthometry: The art or practice of constructing verse correctly; the laws of correct versification.
orthopny: A species of asthma in which respiration can be performed on in an erect posture.
ortive: Rising, or eastern. The ortive amplitude of a planet is an arc of the horizon intercepted between the point where a star rises, and the east point of the horizon, the point where the horizon and equator intersect.
ortolan: A bird of the genus Emberiza, about the size of the lark, with black wings. It is found in France and Italy, feeds on panic grass, and is delicious food.
oryctognosy: That branch of mineralogy which has for its object the classification of minerals, according to well ascertained characters, and under appropriate denominations. Oryctognosy consists in the description of minerals, the determination of their nomenclature, and the systematic arrangement of their different species. It coincides nearly with mineralogy, in its modern acceptation.
oryctography: That part of natural history in which fossils are described.
oryctology: That part of physics which treats of fossils.
oscheocele: A rupture in the scrotum; scrotal hernia.
oscillate: To swing; to move backward and forward; to vibrate.
oscillation: Vibration; a moving backward and forward, or swinging like a pendulum.
oscillatory: Moving backward and forward like a pendulum; swinging; as an oscillatory motion.
oscitancy: The act of gaping or yawning.
Unusual sleepiness; drowsiness; dullness. It might proceed from the oscitancy of transcribers.
oscitation: The act of yawning or gaping from sleepiness.
osculation: In geometry, the contract between any given curve and its osculatory circle, that is, a circle of the same curvature with the given curve.
osculatory: An osculatory circle, in geometry, is a circle having the same curvature with any curve at any given point.
In church history, a tablet or board, with the picture of Christ or the virginwhich is kissed by the priest and then delivered to the people for the same purpose.
osier: o’sher. A willow or water willow, or the twig of the willow, used in making baskets.
osmazome: A substance of an aromatic flavor, obtained from the flesh of the ox.
osmium: A metal recently discovered, and contained in the ore of platinum. A native alloy of this metal with iridium is found in grains along the rivers in South America. Osmium has a dark gray color; it is not volatile when heated in close vessels, but heated in the open air, it absorbs oxygen and forms a volatile oxyd. It is insoluble in the acids, readily soluble in potassa and very volatile. It takes its name from the singular smell of its oxyd.
osmund: A plant, or a genus of plants, osmundaThe most remarkable species is the osmund royal or flowering fern, growing in marshes, the root of which boiled, is very slimy, and is used in stiffening linen.
osnaburg: oz’nburg. A species of coarse linen imported from Osnaburg, in Germany.
ospray: The sea-eagle, a fowl of the genus Falco or hawk, of the size of a peacock. This is our fish hawk. It feeds on fish which it takes by suddenly darting upon them, when near the surface of the water.
osselet: A hard substance growing on the inside of a horse’s knee, among the small bones.
ossific: Having power to ossify or change carneous and membranous substances to bone.
ossification: The change or process of changing from flesh or other matter of animal bodies into a bony substance; as the ossification of an artery.
The formation of bones in animals.
ossified: Converted into bone, or a hard substance like bone.
ossifrage: The ospray or sea-eagle. In Leviticus 11:13, it denotes a different fowl.
ossify: To form bone; to change from a soft animal substance into bone, or convert into a substance of the hardness of bones. This is done by the deposition of calcarious phosphate or carbonate on the part.
To become bone; to change from soft matter into a substance of bony hardness.
ossivorous: Feeding on bones; eating bones; as ossivorous quadrupeds.
ossuary: A charnel house; a place where the bones of the dead are deposited.
ostensibility: The quality or state of appearing or being shown.
ostensible: That may be shown; proper or intended to be shown.

Appearing; seeming; shown, declared or avowed. We say, the ostensible reason or motive for a measure may be the real one, or very different from the real one. This is the common, and I believe the only sense in which the word is used in America. One of the ostensible grounds on which the proprietors had obtained their charter - .
ostensibly: In appearance; in a manner that is declared or pretended. An embargo and non-intercourse which totally defeat the interests they are ostensible destined to promote.
ostensive: Showing; exhibiting. Ostensive demonstration, is one which plainly and directly demonstrates the truth of a proposition.
ostent: A prodigy; a portent; any thing ominous.
ostentate: To make an ambitious display of; to show or exhibit boastingly.
ostentation: Ambitious display; vain show; display of any thing dictated by vanity, or intended to invite praise or flattery. Ostentation of endowments is made by boasting or self-commendation. Ostentation often appears in works of art and sometimes in acts of charity. He knew that good and bountiful minds are sometimes inclined to ostentation. The painter is to make no ostentation of the means by which he strikes the imagination.
ostentatious: Making a display from vanity; boastful; fond of presenting one’s endowments or works to others in an advantageous light. Your modesty is so far from being ostentatious of the good you do
ostentator: One who makes a vain show; a boaster.
osteocol: A carbonate of lime, a fossil formed by incrustation on the stem of a plant. It is found in long, thick, and irregular cylindric pieces, generally hollow, sometimes filled with calcarious earth, and in size, from that of a crow’s quill to that of a man’s arm. It is always found in sand. That word takes its name from an opinion that it has the quality of uniting fractured bones.
osteocolla: A carbonate of lime, a fossil formed by incrustation on the stem of a plant. It is found in long, thick, and irregular cylindric pieces, generally hollow, sometimes filled with calcarious earth, and in size, from that of a crow’s quill to that of a man’s arm. It is always found in sand. That word takes its name from an opinion that it has the quality of uniting fractured bones.
osteocope: Pain in the bones; a violent fixed pain in any part of a bone.
osteologer: One who describes the bones of animals.
osteologist: One who describes the bones of animals.
osteologic: Pertaining to a description of the bones.
osteological: Pertaining to a description of the bones.
osteology: A description of the bones; that part of anatomy which treats of the bones.
ostiary: The mouth or opening by which a river discharges its waters into the sea, or into a lake.
ostmen: East men; Danish settlers in Ireland, so called.
ostracism: In Grecian antiquity, banishment by the people of Athens, of a person whose merit and influence gave umbrage to them. It takes this name from the shell on which the name or the note of acquittal or condemnation was written. It is however most probable that this shell was a piece of baked earth, rendered by the Latins testa.
Banishment; expulsion; separation. Sentenced to a perpetual ostracism from the esteem and confidence, and honors and emoluments of his country.
ostracite: An oyster shell in its fossil state, or a stone formed in the shell, the latter being dissolved. This stone is found in many parts of England, and has been in repute for its efficacy in cases of the gravel.
ostracize: To banish by the popular voice, particularly a person eminent for public services, but who has lost his popularity.
ostrich: A fowl now considered as constituting a distinct genus, the Struthio. This is the largest of all fowls, being four feet high from the ground to the top of the back and seven, eight, and it is said even ten to the top of the head, when standing erect. Its thighs and the sides of the body are naked, and the wings are so short as to be unfit for flying. The plumage is elegant, and much used in ornamental and showy dress. The speed of this fowl in running exceeds that of the fleetest horse.
otacoustic: Assisting the sense of hearing; as an otacoustic instrument.
other: Not the same; different; not this or these. Then the other company which is left shall escape. Genesis 32:8. Behold, it was turned again as his other flesh. Exodus 4:7. Other lords besides thee have had dominion over us. Isaiah 26:13. There is one God, and there is none other but he. Mark 12:32.
Not this, but the contrary; as, on this side of the river stands Troy, on the other side stands Albany. Whosoever shall smite thee on thy right cheek, turn to him the other also. Matthew 5:39.
Noting something besides. To the knowledge of the Latin and Greek, join as much other learning as you can.
Correlative to each, and applicable to any number of individuals. They asked each other of their welfare. Exodus 18:7.
Opposed to some; as,”some fell among thorns - but other fell into good ground.” Matthew 13:7-8.
otherwhere: In some other place; or in other places.
otherwise: In a different manner. Thy father was a worthy prince, and merited, alas! a better fate; but heaven thought otherwise.
By other causes. Sir John Norris failed in the attempt of Lisborn, and returned with the loss, by sickness and otherwise, of 8000 men.
In other respects. It is said truly, that the best men otherwise, are not always the best in regard to society.
otomo: A fowl of the Lagopus kind, about the size of a tame pigeon, a native of Germany, and highly esteemed for food.
otter: The essential oil or essence of roses.

A quadruped of the genus Mustela, nearly two feet in length, of a brown color, with short legs, amphibious and feeding on fish. It burrows in the banks of rivers and ponds, and its toes being webbed, it swims with great rapidity. There are several other species, of which the sea otter is the largest, being about three feet in length.
The name of a coloring substance.
ottoman: Designating something that pertains to the Turks or to their government; as the ottoman power or empire. The word originated in Othman or Osman, the name of a sultan who assumed the government about the year 1300.
ouch: A bezil or socket in which a precious stone or seal is set. Exodus 39:6.
The blow given by a boar’s tusk.
ought: To be held or bound in duty or moral obligation. These ought ye to have done, and not to leave the other undone. Matthew 23:23. We that are strong ought to bear the infirmities of the weak. Romans 15:1. Thou oughtest therefore to have put my money to the exchangers. Matthew 25:27.
To be necessary; to behoove. Ought not Christ to have suffered these things and to enter into glory? Luke 24:26.
To be fit or expedient in a moral view. My brethren, these things ought not so to be. James 3:10.
As a participle, owed; been indebted to. The love and duty I long have ought you. That followed, sir, which to myself I ought.
ounce: A weight, the twelfth part of a pound troy, and the sixteenth of a pound avoirdupois. In troy weight, the ounce is 20 pennyweights, each of 24 grains.
An animal of the genus Felis.
ouphe: oof’y. A fairy; a goblin; an elf. Obs.
our: Pertaining or belonging to us; as our country; our rights; our troops.

Ours, which is primarily the possessive case of our, is never used as an adjective, but as a substitute for the adjective and the noun to which it belongs. Your house is on a plain; ours is on a hill. This is good English, but certainly ours must be the nominative to is, or it has none. Their organs are better disposed than ours for receiving grateful impressions from sensible objects. Here ours stands in the place of our organs, and cannot, in conformity with any rule of construction, be in the possessive case. The same thing was done by them in suing in their courts, which is now done by us in suing in ours.
ourself: This is added after we and us, and sometimes is used without either for myself, in the regal style only, as, we ourself will follow. - Unless we would denude ourself of all force to defend us.
ourselves: of ourself. We or us, not others; added to we, by way of emphasis or opposition. We ourselves might distinctly number in words a great deal farther than we usually do. Safe in ourselves, while on ourselves we - stand.
ousel: 00’zl. The blackbird, a species of the genus Turdus.
ouster: Amotion of possession; disseizin; dispossession; ejection. Ouster of the freehold is effected by abatement, intrusion, disseizin, discontinuance or deforcement. A delivery of lands out of the bands of a guardian, or out of the king’s hands; or a judgment given for that purpose.
out: Without; on the outside; not within; on the exterior or beyond the limits of any inclosed place or given line; opposed to in or within; as, to go out and come in; to rush out.
Abroad; not at home. The master of the house is out; a colloquial phrase for gone out.
In a state of disclosure or discovery. The secret is out, that is, has come out, is disclosed. We shall find out the rogue.
Not concealed. When these are gone, the woman will be out.
In a state of extinction. The candle or the fire is out.
outact: To do beyond; to exceed in act. He has made me heir to treasures, would make me outact a read widow’s whining.
outbalance: To out weigh; to exceed in weight or effect. Let dull Ajax bear away my right, when all his days outbalance this one night.
outbar: To shut out by bars or fortification. These to outbar with painful pionings.
outbid: To bid more than another; to offer a higher price. For Indian spices, for Peruvian gold, prevent the greedy and outbid the bold.
outbound: Destined or proceeding from a country or harbor to a distant country or port; as an outbound ship.
outbrave: To bear down by more daring or insolent conduct. I would outstare the sternest eyes that look, outbrave the heart most daring on the earth, to win thee.
To exceed in splendid appearance. The towers as well as men outbrave the sky.
outbrazen: To bear down with a brazen face or impudence.
outbreak: A bursting forth; eruption. The flash and outbreak of a fiery mind.
outbreathe: To weary by having better breath.
outbuild: outbild’. To exceed in building, or in durability of building.
outcast: or a. Cast out; thrown away; rejected as useless.
One who is cast out or expelled; an exile; one driven from home or country. Isaiah 16:3-4.
outcept: for except, is not in use.
outcry: A vehement or loud cry; cry of distress.
outdo: pret. outdid; pp. outdone. To excel; to surpass; to perform beyond another. An imposture outdoes the original. I grieve to be outdone by Gay.
outer: Being on the outside; external; opposed to inner; as the outer wall; the outer part of a thing; the outer court or gate.
outermost: Being on the extreme external part; remotest from the midst; as the outermost row.
outface: To brave; to bear down with an imposing front or with impudence; to stare down.
outfall: A fall of water; a canal.
outfawn: To exceed in fawning or adulation.
outfit: A fitting out, as of a ship for a voyage; usually in the plural, outfits, the expenses of equipping and furnishing a ship for a voyage.
outflank: To extend the flank of one army beyond that of another.
outely: To fly faster than another; to advance before in flight or progress.
outfrown: To frown down; to overbear by frowning.
outgeneral: To exceed in generalship; to gain advantage over by superior military skill.
outgo: To go beyond; to advance before in going; to go faster.
outgoing: The state of going out. Psalm 65:8.
outgrow: To grow too great or too old for any thing. Children outgrow their garments, and men outgrow their usefulness.
outguard: A guard at a distance from the main body of an army; or a guard at the farthest distance; any thing for defense placed at a distance from the thing to be defended.
outherod: To surpass in enormity, absurdity or cruelty.
outhouse: A small house or building at a little distance from the main house.
outknave: é outna’ve. To surpass in knavery.
outlander: A foreigner; not a native. Obs.
outlandish: Foreign; not native. Nevertheless, even him did outlandish women cause to sin. Nehemiah 13:26.
Born or produced in the interior country, or among rude people; hence, vulgar; rustic; rude; clownish.
outlast: To last longer than something else; to exceed in duration. Candles laid in bran will outlast others of the same stuff.
outlaw: A person excluded from the benefit of the law, or deprived of its protection. Formerly any person might kill an outlaw; but it is now held unlawful for any person to put to death an outlaw, except the sheriff, who has a warrant for that purpose.
To deprive of the benefit and protection of law; to proscribe.
outlawed: Excluded from the benefit of law.
outlawing: Depriving of the benefit of law.
outlawry: The putting a man out of the protection of law, or the process by which a man is deprived of that protection; the punishment of a man who when called into court, contemptuously refuses to appear.
outlay: A laying out or expending; expenditure.
outleap: To leap beyond; to pass by leaping.
outlet: Passage outward; the place or the means by which any thing escapes or is discharged. A gate is the outlet of a city or fort. The mouth of a river is its outlet. Colonies are the outlets of a populous nation.
outlicker: In ships, a small piece of timber fastened to the top of the poop.
outlier: One who does not reside in the place with which his office or duty connects him.
outline: Contour; the line by which a figure is defined; the exterior line.
To draw the exterior line; to delineate; to sketch.
outlive: To live beyond; to survive; to live after something has ceased; as, a man may outlive his children; a person may outlive his estate, his fame and his usefulness. They live too long who happiness outlive.
To live better or to better purpose.
outlying: Lying or being at a distance from the main body or design.
Being on the exterior or frontier.
outmarch: To march faster than; to march so as to leave behind. The horse outmarched the foot.
outmeasure: outmezh’ur. To exceed in measure or extent.
outmost: Farthest outward; most remote from the middle.
outnumber: To exceed in number. The troops outnumbered those of the enemy.
outparish: A parish lying without the walls, or on the border.
outpart: A part remote from the center or main part.
outpass: To pass beyond; to exceed in progress.
outpost: A post or station without the limits of a camp, or at a distance from the main body of an army.
The troops placed at such a station.
outpour: To pour out; to send forth in a stream.
outpray: To exceed in prayer or in earnestness of entreaty.
outpreach: To surpass in preaching; to produce more effect in inculcating lessons or truth. And for a villain’s quick conversion a pill’ry can outpreach a parson.
outprize: To exceed in value or estimated worth.
outrage: To treat with violence and wrong; to abuse by rude or insolent language; to injure by rough, rude treatment of any kind. Base and insolent minds outrage men, when they have hopes of doing it without a return. This interview outrages all decency.
To commit exorbitances; to be guilty of violent rudeness.

Injurious violence offered to persons or things; excessive abuse; wanton mischief. Rude abusive language, scurrility, or opprobrious and contemptuous words, may be an outrage to persons, or to decency and civility. A violent attack upon person or property is an outrage. He wrought great outrages, wasting all the country where he went.
outrageous: Violent; furious; exorbitant; exceeding all bounds of moderation; as outrageous villainies; outrageous talk; outrageous abuse.
Excessive; exceeding reason or decency; as outrageous panegyric.
outre: ootra’y. Being out of the common course or limits; extravagant.
outreason: To excel or surpass in reasoning.
outreign: To reign through the whole of.
outride: To pass by riding; to ride faster than.
To travel about on horseback, or in a vehicle.
outrider: A summoner whose office is to cite men before the sheriff.
One who travels about on horseback.
outrigger: In seamen’s language, a strong beam fixed on the side of a ship and projecting from it, in order to secure the masts in the operation of careening, by counteracting the strain it suffers from the effort of the careening tackle; also, a boom occasionally used in the tops to thrust out the breast back-stays to windward, to increase the angle of tension, and give additional security to the topmast.
outrun: To exceed in running; to leave behind in running.
To exceed; as, to outrun one’s income.
outsail: To sail faster than; to leave behind in sailing.
outscorn: To bear down or confront by contempt; to despise.
outsell: To exceed in amount of sales.
To exceed in the prices of things sold.
outset: Beginning; first entrance on any business. Every thing almost depends upon giving a proper direction to this outset of life.
outshine: To send forth brightness or luster.
To excel in luster or excellence; as Homer outshines all other poets.
outside: The external part of a thing; the part, end or side which forms the surface or superficies.
Superficial appearance; exterior; as the outside of a man or of manners. Created beings see nothing but our outside.
The part or place that lies without or beyond an inclosure. I threw open the door of my chamber and found the family standing on the outside.
outsit: To sit beyond the time of any thing.
outspeak: To speak something beyond; to exceed.
outsport: To sport beyond; to outdo in sporting.
outspread: To extend; to spread; to diffuse.
outstand: To resist effectually; to withstand; to sustain without yielding.
To stand beyond the proper time.
To project outwards from the main body.
outstanding: Not collected; unpaid; as outstanding debts. The whole amount of revenues - as well outstanding as collected.
outstare: To face down; to browbeat; to outface with effrontery; as we say, to stare out of countenance.
outstep: To step or go beyond; to exceed.
outstorm: To overbear by storming. Insults the tempest and outstorms the skies.
outstreet: A street in the extremities of a town.
outstretch: To extend; to stretch or spread out; to expand.
outstrip: To outgo; to outrun; to advance beyond.
outswear: To exceed in swearing; to overpower by swearing.
outswell: To overflow; to exceed in swelling.
outtalk: outtauk’. To overpower by talking; to exceed in talking.
outtongue: outtung’. To bear down by talk, clamor or noise.
outvalue: To exceed in price or value.
outvoice: outvois’. To exceed in roaring or clamor.
outvote: To exceed in the number of votes given; to defeat by plurality of suffrages.
outwalk: To walk faster than; to leave behind in walking.
To exceed the walking of a specter.
outwall: The exterior wall of a building or fortress.
outward: External; exterior; forming the superficial part; as the outward coat of an onion; an outward garment.
External; visible; opposed to inward; as outward hate.
Extrinsic; adventitious. And outward honor for an inward toil.
Foreign; not intestine; as an outward war.
Tending to the exterior part. The fire will force its outward way.
outward-bound: Proceeding from a port or country.
outwardly: Externally; opposed to inwardly; as outwardly content, but inwardly uneasy.
In appearance; not sincerely. Many may inwardly reverence the goodness which they outwardly seem to despise.
outwash: To wash out; to cleanse from.
outwear: To pass tediously to the end. By the stream, if I the night outwear - .
To last longer than something else.
outweed: To weed out; to extirpate, as a weed.
outweigh: To exceed in value, influence or importance. One self-approving hour whole years outweighs of stupid starers and of loud huzzas.
outwind: To extricate by winding; to unloose.
outwing: To move faster on the wing; to outstrip.
outwit: To surpass in design or stratagem; to overreach; to defeat or frustrate by superior ingenuity.
outwork: The part of a fortification most remote from the main fortress or citadel.
outwrest: outrest’. To extort; to draw from or forth by violence.
outwrite: é outri’te. To surpass in writing.
outwrought: outraut’. Outdone; exceeded in act or efficacy.
oval: Of the shape or figure of an egg; oblong; curvilinear; resembling the longitudinal section of an egg. It is sometimes synonymous with elliptical; but an ellipsis is equally broad at both ends, and is not strictly egg-shaped.
Pertaining to eggs; done in the egg; as oval conceptions.
A body or figure in the shape of an egg.
ovarious: Consisting of eggs; as ovarious food.
ovary: The part of a female animal in which the eggs are formed or lodged; or the part in which the fetus is supposed to be formed.
ovate-lanceolate: Having something of the form of an egg and a lance, inclining to the latter.
ovate-subulate: Having something of the form of an egg and an awl, but most tending to the latter.
ovation: In Roman antiquity, a lesser triumph allowed to commanders who had conquered without blood, or defeated an inconsiderable enemy.
ovato-oblong: Oblong in the shape of an egg, or with the end lengthened.
oven: uv’n. An arch of brick or stone work, for baking bread and other things for food. Ovens are made in chimneys or set in the open air.
over: Across; from side to side; implying a passing or moving either above the substance or thing, or on the surface of it. Thus we say, a dog leaps over a stream, or over a table; a boat sails over a lake.
Above in place or position; opposed to below; as the clouds over our heads. The smoke rises over the citys . The mercy-seat that is over the testimony. Exodus 30:6.
Above, denoting superiority in excellence, dignity or value; as the advantages which the christian world has over the heathen. Young Pallas shone conspicuous o’er the rest.
Above in authority, implying the right or power of superintending or governing; opposed to under. Thou shalt be over my house. Genesis 41:40. I will make thee ruler over many things. Vatthew 25:23.
Upon the surface or whole surface; through the whole extent; as, to wander over the earth; to walk over a field, or over a city.
overabound: To abound more than enough; to be superabundant.
overact: To act or perform to excess; as, he overacted his part.
To act more than is necessary.
overagitate: To agitate or discuss beyond what is expedient.
overarch: To arch over; to cover with an arch. Brown with o’erarching shades.
overawe: overaw’. To restrain by awe, fear or superior influence. The kind was present in person to overlook that magistrates and overawe the subjects with the terror of his sword.
overbalance: To weigh down; to exceed in weight, value or importance. The evils which spring from vice overbalance all its pleasures.
Excess of weight or value; something more than an equivalent; as an overbalance of exports; an overbalance of probabilities.
overbear: To bear down; to repress; to subdue. The point of reputation, when the news first came of the battle lost, did overbear the reason of war. Yet fortune, valor, all is overborne by numbers. Till overborne with weight the Cyprians fell.
overbearing: Bearing down; repressing. 2.a. Haughty and dogmatical; disposed or tending to repress or subdue by insolence or effrontery.
overbend: To bend or stretch to excess.
overbid: To bid or offer more than an equivalent.
overblow: To blow with too much violence; a seaman’s phrase.
To blow over, or be past its violence.
To blow away; to dissipate by wind.
overblown: Blown by and gone; blown away; driven by; past. And when this cloud of sorrow’s overblown. VN\ .z
overboard: Literally, over the side of a ship; hence, out of a ship or from on board; as, to fall overboard; which of course is to fall into the water.
overburden: To load with too great weight.
overbuy: To buy at too dear a rate.
overcanopy: To cover as with a canopy.
overcarry: To carry too far; to carry or urge beyond the proper point.
overcast: To cloud; to darken; to cover with gloom. The clouds that overcast our morn shall fly.
To cast or compute at too high a rate; to rate too high. The king in his account of peace and calms did much overcast his fortunes - .
Clouded; overspread with clouds or gloom. The dawn is overcast. Our days of age are sad and overcast.
overcharge: To charge or load to excess; to cloy; to oppress. The heavy load of abundance with which we overcharge nature
To fill to excess; to surcharge; as, to overcharge the memory.
To load with too great a charge, as a gun.
To charge too much; to enter in an account more than is just.
overcloud: To cover or overspread with clouds.
overcome: To conquer; to vanquish; to subdue; as, to overcome enemies in battle.
To surmount; to get the better of; as, to overcome difficulties or obstacles.
To come upon; to invade. J .
To gain the superiority; to be victorious. Romans 3:4.
overcount: To rate above the true value.
overdate: To date beyond the proper period.
overdo: To do or perform too much.
To harass; to fatigue; to oppress by too much action or labor.
To boil, bake or road too much.
To labor too hard; to do too much.
overdone: Wearied or oppressed by too much labor.
Boiled, baked or roasted too much.
overdress: To dress to excess; to adorn too much.
overdrive: To drive too hard, or beyond strength. Genesis 33:13.
overeager: Too eager; too vehement in desire.
overfall: A cataract; the fall of a river.
overfill: To fill to excess; to surcharge.
overflourish: overflurish. To make excessive display or flourish.
overflow: To spread over, as water; to inundate; to cover with water or other fluid.
To deluge; to overwhelm; to cover, as with numbers. The northern nations overflowed all christendom.
To run over; to swell and run over the brim or banks.
To be abundant; to abound; to exuberate; as overflowing plenty.
overflowing: Spreading over, as a fluid; inundating; running over the brim or banks.
overflushed: Flushed to excess; reddened to excess.
overefly: To pass over or cross by flight.
overforwardness: Too great forwardness or readiness; officiousness.
overfreight: overfra’te. To load too heavily; to fill with too great quantity or numbers; as, to overtreight a boat.
overgird: To gird or bind too closely.
overglance: To glance over; to run over with the eye.
overgrassed: Overstocked with grass; overgrown with grass.
overgrow: To cover with growth or herbage.
To grow beyond; to rise above.
To grow beyond the fit or natural size; as a hugh overgrown ox.
overhandle: To handle too much; to mention too often.
overharden: To harden too much; to make too hard.
overhaul: To turn over for examination; to separate and inspect.
To gain upon in a chase; to overtake.
overhead: overhed’. Aloft; above; in the zenith or ceiling.
overhear: To hear by accident; to hear what is not addressed to the hearer, or not intended to be heard by him.
overjoy: To give great joy to; to transport with gladness.
