overlabor: To execute with too much care.
overlade: To load with too great a cargo or other burden.
overlaid: Oppressed with weight; smothered; covered over.
overlay: To lay too much upon; to oppress with incumbent weight; as a country overlaid with inhabitants. Our sins have overlaid our hopes.
To cover to spread over the surface; as, to overlay capitals of columns with silver; cedar overlaid with gold.
To smother with close covering; as, to overlay an infant.
To overwhelm; to smother. A heap of ashes that o’er lays your fire.
To cloud; to overcast. - As when a cloud his beam doth overlay.
overleap: To leap over; to pass or move from side to side by leaping; as, to overleap a ditch or a fence.
overleather: The leather which forms or is intended to form the upper part of a shoe; that which is over the foot.
overlether: The leather which forms or is intended to form the upper part of a shoe; that which is over the foot.
overleaven: To leaven too much; to cause to rise and swell too much.
To mix too much with; to corrupt.
overliberal: Too liberal; too free; abundant to excess; as overliberal diet.
overlive: overliv’. To outlive; to live longer than another; to survive.
overliver: One that lives longest; a survivor.
overload: To load with too heavy a burden or cargo; to fill to excess; as, to overload the stomach or a vehicle.
overlook: To view from a higher place; applied to persons; as, to stand ona hill and overlook a city: .
To stand in a more elevated place, or to rise so high as to afford the means of looking down on; applied to things. The tower overlooked the town.
To see from behind or over the shoulder of another; to see from a higher position; as, to overlook a paper when one is writing.
To inspect; to superintend; to oversee; implying care and watchfulness. He was present in person to overlook the magistrates.
overlove: To love to excess; to prize or value too much.
overmast: To furnish with a mast or with masts that are too long or too heavy for the weight of keel.
overmasted: Having masts too long or too heavy for the ship.
overmaster: To overpower; to subdue; to vanquish; to govern.
overmatch: To be too powerful for; to conquer; to subdue; to oppress by superior force.
One superior in power; one able to overcome.
overmeasure: overmezh’ur. To measure or estimate too largely.
overmezh’ur. Excess of measure; something that exceeds the measure proposed.
overmost: Highest; over the rest in authority.
overmuch: Too much; exceeding what is necessary or proper.
overname: To name over or in a series.
overoffice: To lord by virtue of an office.
overofficious: Too busy; too ready to intermeddle; too importunate.
overpaint: To color or describe too strongly.
overpass: To overlook; to pass without regard.
To omit; not to receive or include.
overpassed: Passed by; passed away; gone; past.
overpast: Passed by; passed away; gone; past.
overpay: To pay too much or more than is due.
To reward beyond the price or merit.
overperch: To perch over or above; to fly over.
overpersuade: To persuade or influence against one’s inclination or opinion.
overpicture: To exceed the representation or picture.
overplus: Surplus; that which remains after a supply, or beyond a quantity proposed. Take what is wanted and return the overplus. It would look like a fable to report that this gentleman gives away all which is the overplus of a great fortune.
overply: To ply to excess; to exert with too much vigor.
overpower: To affect with a power or force that cannot be borne; as, the light overpowers the eyes.
To vanquish by force; to subdue; to reduce to silence in action or submission; to defeat.
overpress: To bear upon with irresistible force; to crush; to overwhelm.
overprize: To value or prize at too high a rate.
overprompt: Too prompt; too ready or eager.
overproportion: To make of too great proportion.
overrake: To break in upon a ship. When the waves break in upon a ship riding at anchor, it is said, they overrake her, or she is overraked.
overrate: To rate at too much; to estimate at a value or amount beyond the truth.
overreach: To reach beyond in any direction; to rise above; to extend beyond.
To deceive by cunning, artifice or sagacity; to cheat.
Applied to horses, to strike the toe of the hind foot against the heel or shoe of the fore foot.
The act of striking the heel of the fore foot with the toe of the hind foot.
overreacher: One that overreaches; one that deceives.
overreaching: The act of deceiving; a reaching too far.
overred: To smear with a red color.
override: To ride too much; to ride beyond the strength of the horse.
overrule: To influence or control by predominant power; to subject to superior authority. The law must overrule all private opinions of right and wrong. His passion and animosity overruled his conscience.
In law, to supersede or reject; as, the plea was overruled by the court.
overruler: One who controls, directs or governs.
overruling: Controlling; subjecting to authority. 2.a. Exerting superior and controlling power; as an overruling Providence.
overrun: To run or spread over; to grow over; to cover all over. The sluggard’s farm is overrun with weeds. Some plants unchecked will soon overrun a field. The Canada thistle is overrunning the northern parts of New England, as it has overrun Normandy.
To march or rove over; to harass by hostile incursions; to ravage. The south of Europe was formerly overrun by the Goths, Vandals and other barbarians.
To outrun; to run faster than another and leave him behind. Ahimaaz ran by the way of the plain, and overran Cushi. 2 Samuel 18:23.
Among printers, to change the disposition of types and carry those of one line into another, either in correction, or in the contraction or extension of columns.
overwrought: Worked all over; as overwrought with ornaments.
overzealed: Too much excited with zeal; ruled by too much zeal.
overzealous: overzel’ous. Too zealous; eager to excess.
oviduct: In animals, a passage for the egg from the ovary to the womb, or a passage which conveys the egg from the ovary.
oviform: Having the form or figure of an egg.
ovine: Pertaining to sheep; consisting of sheep.
oviparous: Producing eggs, or producing young from eggs. Fowls and reptiles are oviparous animals.
ovoid: Having the shape of an egg.
ovolo: In architecture, a round molding, the quarter of a circle; called also the quarter round.
owe: To be indebted; to be obliged or bound to pay. The merchants owe a large sum to foreigners. A son owes help and honor to his father. One was brought to him who owed him ten thousand talents. Matthew 18:24. Owe no man any thing, but to love one another. Romans 13:8.
To be obliged to ascribe to; to be obliged for; as, that he may owe to me all his deliverance.
To possess; to have; to be the owner of. Thou dost here usurp the name thou owest not.
To be due or owing. O deem thy fall not ow’d to man’s decree.
owing: Due; that moral obligation requires to be paid; as the money owing to a laborer for services, or to another country for goods.
Consequential; ascribable to, as the cause. Misfortunes are often owing to vices or miscalculations.
Imputable to as an agent. His recovery from sickness is owing less to his physician, than to the strength of his constitution.
owl: A fowl of the genus Strix, that flies chiefly in the night.
owling: The offense of transporting wool or sheep out of England, contrary to the statute.
owl-like: Like an owl in look and habits.
own: Belonging to; possessed; peculiar; usually expressing property with emphasis, or in express exclusion of others. It follows my, your, his, their, thyGod created man in his own image. Adam begat a son in his own likeness. Let them fall by their own counsel. He washed us from our sins in his own blood. In the phrases, his own nations, his own country, the word own denotes that the person belongs to the nation or country.
Own often follows a verb; as, the book is not my own, that is, my own book. OWIN.
It is used as a substitute. That they may dwell in a place of their own. 2 Samuel 7:10. In this use, a noun cannot follow own.
“He came to his own, and his own received him not,” that is, his own nation or people; own being here used as a substitute, like many other adjectives.&

To have the legal or rightful title to; to have the exclusive right of possession and use. A free holder in the United states owns his farm. Men often own land or goods which are not in their possession.
owned: The legal title being vested in; as, the property is owned by a company.
owner: The rightful proprietor; one who has the legal or rightful title, whether he is the possessor or not. The ox knoweth his owner. Isaiah 7:3. The centurion believed the master and owner of the ship. Acfs 27:11.
ownership: Property; exclusive right of possession; legal or just claim or title. The ownership of the estate is in A; the possession is in B.
owning: Having the legal or just title to.
owse: Bark of oak beaten or ground to small pieces.
owser: Bark and water mixed in a tan-pit.
ox: oxen. pron. ox’n. The male of the bovine genus of quadrupeds, castrated and grown to his size or nearly so. The young male is called in America a steer. The same animal not castrated is called a bull. These distinctions are well established with us in regard to domestic animals of this genus. When we speak of wild animals of this kind, ox is sometimes applied both to the male and female, and in zoology, the same practice exists in regard to the domestic animals. Sop in common usage, a pair of bulls yoked may be sometimes called oxen. We never apply the name ox to the cow or female of the domestic kind. Oxen in the plural may comprehend both the male and female. x.
oxalate: In chimistry, a salt formed by a combination of the oxalic acid with a base.
oxalic: Pertaining to sorrel. The oxalic acid is the acid of sorrel.
ox-eye: A plant of the genus Buphthalmum; another of the genus Anthemis; also, the ox-eye daisy or Chrysanthemum.
ox-eyed: Having large full eyes, like those of an ox.
oxfly: A fly hatched under the skin of cattle.
oxgang: In ancient laws, as much land as an ox can plow ina year; said to be fifteen acres, or as others allege, twenty acres.
oxiodic: Pertaining to or consisting of the compound of oxygen and iodine.
oxstall: A stall or stand for oxen.
oxtongue: ox’tung. A plant of the genus Picris.
oxycrate: A mixture of water and vinegar.
oxyd: In chimistry, a substance formed by the combination of a portion of oxygen with some base; or a substance combined with oxygen, without being in the state of an acid.
oxydability: The capacity of being converted into an oxyd.
oxydable: Capable of being converted into an oxyd.
oxydate: To convert into an oxyd, as metals and other substances, by combination with oxygen. It differs from acidify, to make acid, or to convert into an acid, as in oxydation the acid that enters into combination is not sufficient to form an acid.
oxydation: The operation or process of converting into an oxyd, as metals or other substances, by combining with them a certain portion of oxygen.
oxygen: In chimistry, oxygen or oxygen gas is an element or substance so named from its property of generating acids; it is the respirable part of air, vital air, or the basis of it; it is called the acidifying principle, and the principle or support of combustion. Modern experiments, however, prove that it is not necessary in all cases to combustion or to acidity. Oxygen is a permanently elastic fluid, invisible, inodorous, and a little heavier than atmospheric air. In union with azote or nitrogen, it forms atmospheric air, of which it constitutes about a fifth part. Water contains about 85% of it, and it exists in most vegetable and animal products, acids, salts and oxyds. It forms 50% of silex, 47 of alumin, 28 of lime, 40 of magnesia, 17 of potash, and 25 of soda.
oxygenate: To unite or cause to combine with oxygen, without the evolution of heat or light; to acidify by oxygen.
oxygenation: The act, operation or process of combining with oxygen.
oxygenous: Pertaining to oxygen, or obtained from it.
oxygon: A triangle having three acute angles.
oxy-iodine: In chimistry, a compound of the chloriodic and oxiodic acids.
oxymel: A mixture of vinegar and honey.
oxymoron: A rhetorical figure, in which an epithet of a quite contrary signification is added to a word; as cruel kindness. Oxyprussic acid, chloroprussic acid. OX YMVORON.
oxyrrhodine: A mixture of two parts of the oil of roses with one of the vinegar of roses.
oyer: In law, a hearing or trial of causes. A court of oyer and terminer is constituted by a commission to inquire, hear and determine all treasons, felonies and misdemeanors.
The hearing, as of a writ, bond, note or other specialty; as when a defendant in court prays oyer of a writing.
oyes: This word is used by the sheriff or his substitute in making proclamation in court, requiring silence and attention. it is thrice repeated, and most absurdly pronounced, O yes.
oyster: A bivalvular testaceous animal, found adhering to rocks or other fixed substances in salt water which is shallow, or in the mouths of rivers. Oysters are deemed nourishing and delicious.
oyster-shell: The hard covering or shell of the oyster.
oyster-wench: A woman whose occupation is to sell oysters; a low woman. P P is the sixteenth letter of the English Alphabet, and a labial articulation formed by a close compression of the anterior part of the lips, as in ep. It is convertible into b and f, sometimes into v. This letter is found int he oriental languages, from which it was received into the Greek and Latin; except however the Arabic, which has not this letter, and the Arabians cannot easily pronounce it. In some words which we have borrowed from the Greek, p is mute, as in psalm, ptisan; but is not silent in English words, unless it may be in receipt, and a few irregular words. P aspirated or followed by h, represents the Greek, which answers to the English f, as in philosophy. P. As an abbreviation, P. stands for Publius, pondoPA. DIG for patricia dignitas; P.C. for Patres Conscripti; P.F. for Publius Fabius; P.P. for propositum publi
oyster-wife: A woman whose occupation is to sell oysters; a low woman. oyster-woman: A woman whose occupation is to sell oysters; a low woman. occipital: Pertaining to the back part of the head, or to the occiput.
occiput: The hinder part of the head, or that part of the skull which forms the hind part of the head.
occision: s as z. A killing; the act of killing.
occlusion: s as z. a shutting up; a closing.
occult: Hidden from the eye or understanding; invisible; secret; unknown; undiscovered; undetected; as the occult qualities of matter. The occult sciences are magic, necromancy. Occult lines, in geometry, are such as are drawn with the compasses or a pencil, and are scarcely visible.
occultation: a hiding; also, the time a star or planet is hid from our sight, when eclipsed by the interposition of the body of a planet.
In astronomy, the hiding of a star or planet from our sight, by passing behind some other of the heavenly bodies.
occultness: the state of being concealed from view; secretness.
occupancy: In law, the taking possession of a thing not belonging to any person. the person who first takes possession of land is said to have or hold it by right of occupancy. Occupancy gave the original right to the property in the substance of the earth itself.
occupant: He that occupies or takes possession; he that has possession.

In law, one that first takes possession of that which has no legal owner. The right of property, either in wild beasts and fowls, or in land belonging to no person, vests in the first occupant. The property in these cases follows the possession.
occupate: To hold; to possess; to take up.
occupation: Possession; a holding or keeping; tenure; use; as lands in the occupation of AB.
That which engages the time and attention; employment; business. He devotes to study all the time that his other occupations will permit.

The principal business of one’s life; vocation; calling; trade; the business which a man follows to procure a living or obtain wealth. Agriculture, manufactures and commerce furnish the most general occupations of life. Painting, statuary, music, are agreeable occupations. Men not engaged in some useful occupation commonly fall into vicious courses.
occupier: One that occupies or takes possession.
One who follows an employment. Ezekiel 27:27.
occupy: To take possession. The person who first occupies land which has no owner, has the right of property.
To keep in possession; to possess; to hold or keep for use. The tenant occupies a farm under a lease of twenty one years. A lodger occupies an apartment; a man occupies the chair in which he sits.

To take up; to possess; to cover or fill. The camp occupies five acres of ground. Air may be so rarefied as to occupy a vast space. The writing occupies a sheet of paper, or it occupies five lines only.
To employ; to use. The archbishop may have occasion to occupy more chaplains than six.
To employ; to busy one’s self. Every man should be occupied, or should occupy himself, in some useful labor.
occur: Primarily, to meet; to strike against; to clash; and so used by Bentley, but this application is obsolete.

To meet or come to the mind; to be presented to the mind, imagination or memory. We say, no better plan occurs to me or to my mind; it does not occur to my recollection; the thought did not occur to me. There doth not occur to me any use of this experiment for profit .
To appear; to meet the eye; to be found here and there. This word occurs in twenty places in the Scriptures; the other word does not occur in a single place; it does not occur in the sense suggested.
occurrence: Literally, a coming or happening; hence, any incident or accidental event; that which happens without being designed or expected; any single event. We speak of an unusual occurrence, or of the ordinary occurrences of life.
Occasional presentation. Voyages detain the mind by the perpetual occurrence and expectation of something new.
occurrent: Incident; any thing that happens. Obs.
occursion: A meeting of bodies; a clash.
ocean: The vast body of water which covers more than three fifths of the surface of the globe, called also the sea, or great sea. It is customary to speak of the ocean as if divided into three parts, the Atlantic ocean, the Pacific ocean, and the Indian ocean; but the ocean is one mass or body, partially separated by the continents of Europe, Asia and Africa on one side, and by America on the other.
An immense expanse; as the boundless ocean of eternity; oceans of duration and space.
o’shun. Pertaining to the main or great sea; as the ocean wave; ocean stream.
ocellated: Formed with the figues of little eyes.
ocher: A variety of clay deeply colored by the oxyd of iron. Its most common colors are red, yellow and brown. It is used as a pigment.
ocherous: Consisting of ocher; as ocherous matter.
Resembling ocher; as an ocherous color.
ochlocracy: A form of government in which the multitude or common people rule.
ocra: A viscous vegetable substance in the West Indies, used in soupsIt is obtained by boiling the green pods of the Hibiscus esculentus. also, the name of the plant itself.
octachord: an instrument or system of eight sounds.
octagon: In geometry, a figure of eight sides and eight angles. When the sides and angles are equal, it is a regular octagon which may be inscribed in a circle.
In fortification, a place with eight bastions.
octagonal: Having eight sides and eight angles.
octahedron: The word is used as a noun or an adjective. We say, an octavo, or an octavo volume. The true phrase is, a book in octavo.
octander: In botany, a plant having eight stamens.
octant: In astronomy, that aspect of two planets in which they are distant from each other the eighth part of a circle or 45 degrees.
octave: The eighth day after a festival.
Eight days together after a festival.

In music, an eighth, or an interval of seven degrees or twelve semitones. The octave is the most perfect of the chords, consisting of six full tones and two semitones major. It contains the whole diatonic scale.
octavo: A book in which a sheet is folded into eight leaves. The word is used as a noun or an adjective. We say, an octavo, or an octavo volume. The true phrase is, a book in octavo.
octennial: In geometry, a solid contained by eight equal and equilateral triangles. it is one of the five regular bodies.
orthodox: Sound in the christian faith; believing the genuine doctrines taught in the Scriptures; opposed to heretical; as an orthodox christian.
According with the doctrines of Scripture; as an orthodox creed or faith,
overrunning: Spreading over; ravaging; changing the disposition of types.
oversee: To superintend; to overlook, implying care.
To pass unheeded; to omit; to neglect.
overseer: One who overlooks; a superintendent; a supervisor.
An officer who has the care of the poor or of an idiot.
overset: To overspread with numbers. Were it not for the ibis, it has been supposed Egypt would be overrun with crocodiles.
To turn from the proper position or basis; to turn upon the side, or to turn bottom upwards; as, to overset a coach, a ship or a building.
To subvert; to overthrow; as, to overset the constitution of a state; to overset a scheme of policy.
To throw off the proper foundation.
To turn or be turned over; to turn or fall off the basis or bottom. A crank vessel is liable to overset.
overshade: To cover with shade; to cover with any thing that causes darkness; to render dark or gloomy.
overshadow: To throw a shadow over; to overshade.z
To shelter; to protect; to cover with protecting influence.
overshadower: One that throws a shade over any thing.
overshot: An overshot wheel is one that receives the water, shot over the top, on the descent. An overshot wheel is moved by less water than an undershot wheel.
oversize: To surpass in bulk or size.
overskip: To skip or leap over; to pass by leaping.
oversleep: To sleep too long; as, to oversleep the usual hour of rising.
overslip: To slip or pass without notice; to pass undone, unnoticed or unused; to omit; to neglect; as, to overslip time or opportunity.
overslow: To render slow; to check; to curb.
oversold: Sold at too high a price.
oversorrow: To grieve or afflict to excess.
overspeak: To speak too much; to use too many words.
overspent: Harassed or fatigued to an extreme degree.
overspread: To pass swiftly over. OVERSHOOT. To overshoot one’s self, to venture too far; to assert too much. OVERSHOOT.
To spread over; to cover over. The deluge overspread the earth.
overspred’. To be spread or scattered over; as, weeds overspread the ground.
overstand: To stand too much on price or conditions; to lose a sale by holding the price too high.
overstep: To step over or beyond; to exceed.
overstock: To fill too full; to crowd; to supply with more than is wanted. The
overstore: To store with too much; to supply or fill with superabundance.
overstrain: To strain to excess; to make too violent efforts.
oversupply: To furnish more than is sufficient.
oversway: To overrule; to bear down; to control.
overswell: To swell or rise above; to overflow.
overt: Open to view; public; apparent; as overt virtues; an overt essay. But the word is now used chiefly in law. Thus an overt act of treason is distinguished from secret design or intention not carried into effect, and even from words spoken. A market overt, is a place where goods are publicly exposed to sale. A pound over, is one open overhead, as distinguished from a pound covert or close.
overtake: To come up with in a course, pursuit, progress or motion; to catch. The enemy said, I will pursue, I will overtake. Exodus 15:9.
To come upon; to fall on afterwards. Vengeance shall overtake the wicked.
To take by surprise. Brethren, if a man be overtaken in a fault, ye who are spiritual, restore such one in the spirit of meekness. Galatians 6:17.
overtask: To impose too heavy a task or injunction on.
overthrow: To turn upside down. His wife overthrew the table.
To ruin; to demolish. When the walls of Thebes he overthrew.
To defeat; to conquer; to vanquish; as, to overthrow an army or an enemy.
To subvert; to destroy; as, to overthrow the constitution or state; to overthrow religion.
overthrower: One that overthrows, defeats or destroys.
overthwart: Opposite; being over the way or street.
overthwartness: The state of being athwart or lying across.
overtire: To tire to excess; to subdue by fatigue.
overtitle: To give too high a title to.
overtop: To obscure; to make of less importance by superior excellence.
overtrip: To trip over; to walk nimbly over.
overtrust: To trust with too much confidence.
overture: Proposal; something offered for consideration, acceptance or rejection. The prince made overtures of peace, which were accepted.

The opening piece, prelude or symphony of some public act, ceremony or entertainment. The overture’ in theatrical entertainments, is a piece of music usually ending in a fugue. The overture of a jubilee is a general procession.
overturn: To overset; to turn or throw from a basis or foundation; as, to overturn a carriage or a building.
To subvert; to ruin; to destroy.
State of being overturned or subverted; overthrow.
overvalue: To rate at too high a price.
overvote: To outvote; to outnumber in votes given.
overwatch: To watch to excess; to subdue by long want of rest.
overweary: To subdue with fatigue. world may be overstocked with inhabitants. The market is often overstocked with goods. OVERSTOCK.
To furnish with more cattle than are wanted; as, to overstock a farm. OVERSTOCK.
To supply with more seed than is wanted; as, to overstock land with clover. OVERSTOCK.
overweather: overweth’er. To bruise or batter by violence of weather.
overween: To think too highly; to think arrogantly or conceitedly.
To reach beyond the truth in thought; to think too favorably.
overweening: Thinking too highly or conceitedly. 2.a. That thinks too highly, particularly of one’s self; conceited; vain; as overweening pride; an overweening brain.
overweeningly: With too much vanity or conceit.
overweigh: To exceed in weight; to cause to preponderate; to outweigh; to overbalance.
overwhelm: To overspread or crush beneath something violent and weighty, that covers or encompasses the whole; as, to overwhelm with waves.
To immerse and bear down; in a figurative sense; as, to be overwhelmed with cares, afflictions or business.
overwing: To outflank; to extend beyond the wing of an army.
overwise: s as z. Wise to affectation.
overwork: To work beyond the strength; to cause to labor too much; to tire.
